extra.reasoning:
opted_out becomes true only via t1 and is never reset, so it stays true forever once set. sent becomes true via t2 (requires not opted_out, so harmless here) or via t5 (on send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=8, sets sent:=true). t5 is the bug: it re-enables sending after opt-out once ticks reaches 8.

Counterexample execution (10 events, within the 12-event bound):
1. optout: t1 fires -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0.
2-9. tick x8: t4 fires each time, ticks goes 0->1->...->8 (capped at 8), sent stays false, opted_out stays true. After 8 ticks ticks=8.
10. send_attempt: now opted_out=true and ticks>=8, so t5 is enabled and fires, setting sent:=true while opted_out is true.

Resulting reachable state: opted_out=true, ticks=8, sent=true. This directly violates the invariant (opted_out implies not sent), so globally(opted_out implies not sent) fails. On the send_attempt at step 10 both t3 (send_attempt if opted_out) and t5 are enabled; under the standard nondeterministic guarded-command semantics any enabled transition may fire, and t5 firing produces the violating state. The property is violated.
